---
title: "Booleans and Conditionals"
description: "Ask yes/no questions and choose different paths with if, elif, and else."
execute:
  echo: true
---

## Why this matters

Ask yes/no questions and choose different paths with if, elif, and else.

This lesson combines related subtopics that belong together in one learning
conversation. You will still pause for a quiz after each section, but you do not
need to jump between separate pages while building one mental model.

::: {.questions}
### Questions

By the end of this lesson, you should be able to answer:

- How do the sections in **Booleans and Conditionals** fit together?
- Which small example demonstrates each section?
- Which debugging clue should I check first for each section?
:::

::: {.learning-objectives}
### Learning objectives

You will practice how to:

- explain the shared concept for this lesson;
- use each section as one step in a larger workflow;
- complete 2 short section quizzes before moving on;
- connect examples, mistakes, and debugging routines.
:::

## Lesson map

- Section 1: **Booleans and Comparisons** — Use True/False values and comparisons to ask questions in code.
- Section 2: **Conditionals** — Choose different actions with if, elif, and else.

<!-- mermaid-visual-model -->
## Visual model

::: {.visual-model}
A conditional is a branching path. Python asks a yes/no question, then runs only
the first block whose condition is true.

```{mermaid}
flowchart TD
  start([Start]) --> q1{"score >= 90?"}
  q1 -- True --> excellent["print('excellent')"]
  q1 -- False --> q2{"score >= 70?"}
  q2 -- True --> passing["print('passing')"]
  q2 -- False --> practice["print('keep practicing')"]
  excellent --> stop([Continue after conditional])
  passing --> stop
  practice --> stop
```

When debugging conditionals, trace the questions in order and stop at the first
true branch.
:::

## Section 1: Booleans and Comparisons

Use True/False values and comparisons to ask questions in code.

::: {.analogy}
### Analogy for this section

A comparison is like a yes/no question on a form: exactly one box should describe the
result.
:::

### Mental model

A Boolean value is either True or False; comparisons produce Booleans.

### Tiny example

Predict what will happen before you run the code.

```python
temperature = 18
print(temperature < 20)
print(temperature == 18)
print(temperature != 30)
```

### Walkthrough

1. `temperature = 18` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
2. `print(temperature < 20)` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
3. `print(temperature == 18)` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
4. `print(temperature != 30)` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.

After running the example, compare the actual output with your prediction. If
they differ, do not erase your prediction. The difference is the part that can
teach you the most.

### Challenge

::: {.challenge}
Change one input value, predict the new output, run the code, and explain the difference
in one sentence.
:::

<details class="solution">
<summary>Show one possible solution path</summary>

1. Copy the tiny example into Colab, Jupyter, or a `.py` file.
2. Mark the line you plan to change.
3. Write a one-sentence prediction.
4. Run the changed code.
5. If the result surprises you, restore the original and change a smaller part.

The goal is not to find the only correct answer. The goal is to create a small
experiment where you can explain cause and effect.

</details>

### Common mistakes

::: {.debugging-corner}
Use == to compare values. Using = inside a condition is not how Python asks equality
questions.
:::

When you get stuck, use this debugging routine:

1. Read the last line of the error message or inspect the unexpected output.
2. Find the smallest line of code that could be responsible.
3. Print or inspect the value and type at that point.
4. Change one thing.
5. Run again and record what changed.

### Section checkpoint

This quiz checks the ideas in this section before you move on.

<script type="application/json" class="fcpython-ojs-quiz-config">
{
  "id": "core-python-booleans-and-comparisons",
  "title": "Booleans and Comparisons checkpoint",
  "instructions": "Choose an answer, then check your work.",
  "questions": [
    {
      "id": "main-idea",
      "prompt": "Which statement best describes Booleans and Comparisons?",
      "options": [
        "A Boolean value is either True or False; comparisons produce Booleans.",
        "It is mainly about memorizing every Python command before writing code.",
        "It is a topic you can safely skip because later tools replace it.",
        "It is only useful after you already know advanced programming."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The key idea is: A Boolean value is either True or False; comparisons produce Booleans."
    },
    {
      "id": "analogy",
      "prompt": "What is the purpose of the analogy in this lesson?",
      "options": [
        "To connect a new programming idea to an everyday experience.",
        "To replace practice with memorization.",
        "To prove that programming is only for experts.",
        "To avoid reading and running code examples."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The analogy in this lesson is: A comparison is like a yes/no question on a form: exactly one box should describe the result."
    },
    {
      "id": "debugging-habit",
      "prompt": "What is the best first debugging habit for this topic?",
      "options": [
        "Read the message or output carefully and change one thing at a time.",
        "Delete the whole program immediately.",
        "Change many lines before running again.",
        "Assume Python is random."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Small experiments make cause and effect visible."
    },
    {
      "id": "practice-habit",
      "prompt": "How should you practice after the tiny example works?",
      "options": [
        "Make one small variation, predict the result, run it, and explain what changed.",
        "Copy a large project without reading it.",
        "Skip practice and rely on recognition.",
        "Only read the final key points."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Prediction plus small variation builds durable understanding."
    }
  ]
}
</script>

{{< include ../_includes/ojs-quiz.qmd >}}

## Section 2: Conditionals

Choose different actions with if, elif, and else.

::: {.analogy}
### Analogy for this section

A conditional is like a fork in a path: the sign decides which branch you walk down.
:::

### Mental model

A conditional runs a block of code only when a condition is true.

### Tiny example

Predict what will happen before you run the code.

```python
score = 82
if score >= 90:
    print("excellent")
elif score >= 70:
    print("passing")
else:
    print("keep practicing")
```

### Walkthrough

1. `score = 82` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
2. `if score >= 90:` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
3. `print("excellent")` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
4. `elif score >= 70:` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
5. `print("passing")` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
6. Continue the same process for the remaining lines, one line at a time.

After running the example, compare the actual output with your prediction. If
they differ, do not erase your prediction. The difference is the part that can
teach you the most.

### Challenge

::: {.challenge}
Change one input value, predict the new output, run the code, and explain the difference
in one sentence.
:::

<details class="solution">
<summary>Show one possible solution path</summary>

1. Copy the tiny example into Colab, Jupyter, or a `.py` file.
2. Mark the line you plan to change.
3. Write a one-sentence prediction.
4. Run the changed code.
5. If the result surprises you, restore the original and change a smaller part.

The goal is not to find the only correct answer. The goal is to create a small
experiment where you can explain cause and effect.

</details>

### Common mistakes

::: {.debugging-corner}
Indentation controls what belongs inside a branch. Misaligned spaces can change program
meaning.
:::

When you get stuck, use this debugging routine:

1. Read the last line of the error message or inspect the unexpected output.
2. Find the smallest line of code that could be responsible.
3. Print or inspect the value and type at that point.
4. Change one thing.
5. Run again and record what changed.

### Section checkpoint

This quiz checks the ideas in this section before you move on.

<script type="application/json" class="fcpython-ojs-quiz-config">
{
  "id": "core-python-conditionals",
  "title": "Conditionals checkpoint",
  "instructions": "Choose an answer, then check your work.",
  "questions": [
    {
      "id": "main-idea",
      "prompt": "Which statement best describes Conditionals?",
      "options": [
        "A conditional runs a block of code only when a condition is true.",
        "It is mainly about memorizing every Python command before writing code.",
        "It is a topic you can safely skip because later tools replace it.",
        "It is only useful after you already know advanced programming."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The key idea is: A conditional runs a block of code only when a condition is true."
    },
    {
      "id": "analogy",
      "prompt": "What is the purpose of the analogy in this lesson?",
      "options": [
        "To connect a new programming idea to an everyday experience.",
        "To replace practice with memorization.",
        "To prove that programming is only for experts.",
        "To avoid reading and running code examples."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The analogy in this lesson is: A conditional is like a fork in a path: the sign decides which branch you walk down."
    },
    {
      "id": "debugging-habit",
      "prompt": "What is the best first debugging habit for this topic?",
      "options": [
        "Read the message or output carefully and change one thing at a time.",
        "Delete the whole program immediately.",
        "Change many lines before running again.",
        "Assume Python is random."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Small experiments make cause and effect visible."
    },
    {
      "id": "practice-habit",
      "prompt": "How should you practice after the tiny example works?",
      "options": [
        "Make one small variation, predict the result, run it, and explain what changed.",
        "Copy a large project without reading it.",
        "Skip practice and rely on recognition.",
        "Only read the final key points."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Prediction plus small variation builds durable understanding."
    }
  ]
}
</script>

{{< include ../_includes/ojs-quiz.qmd >}}

## Notebook and Colab practice

Open a blank notebook at <https://colab.new>. Use one section at a time: copy the
tiny example, predict the result, run it, answer the section quiz, and then move
to the next section. This is better than copying the entire page at once.

## Instructor note

<details class="instructor">
<summary>Teaching notes</summary>

- Treat each section as a short teaching episode.
- Pause for the section quiz before introducing the next section.
- Ask learners to compare sections: what stayed the same, and what changed?
- If time is short, teach the first two sections live and assign the rest as practice.

</details>

## Key points

::: {.key-points}
- **Booleans and Comparisons**: A Boolean value is either True or False; comparisons produce Booleans.
- **Conditionals**: A conditional runs a block of code only when a condition is true.
- Use the section quizzes as gates: review before moving on if a quiz feels uncertain.
:::

## References

- Python Tutorial: https://docs.python.org/3/tutorial/
- Built-in types: https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html
- Quarto OJS documentation: https://quarto.org/docs/interactive/ojs/
- ipywidgets documentation: https://ipywidgets.readthedocs.io/en/stable/
